abstract: Global labor markets are currently navigating profound shifts driven by socio-political instability, rising structural inequality, and persistent employment insecurity. In response, international organizations have stepped forward with policy frameworks designed to safeguard the health and well-being of the global workforce. This article examines the influence of the International Labour Organization’s (ILO) Decent Work Agenda on the strategic priorities of the World Health Organization (WHO) and the World Bank. Drawing on 16 semi-structured interviews with key informants across these three institutions, the study utilizes agenda-setting theory to analyze how the concept of "decent work" gains traction or faces resistance. Our findings reveal that while the Agenda has successfully shaped a unified narrative within the UN system—particularly aligning with the WHO’s health-as-a-human-right framework—it encounters ideological friction at the World Bank, where market-centered terminology like "job creation" is preferred. The analysis highlights that while catalytic events, such as the 2008 global financial crisis, create windows of opportunity for social justice policies, challenges remain regarding the robustness of the underlying evidence base and the Agenda's measurable impact on national-level labor conditions. Underlining the complexities of horizontal policy coordination, this research explores the mechanisms through which global institutions negotiate, compete, and collaborate to define the future of work.
